title: About
text: About this site

title: ssstats: a package for applied statistics courses
text:
ssstats: a package for applied statistics courses




This package was developed for use in applied statistics courses at the University of West Florida. All functions were developed to bring a tidy approach to applied statistics, making R syntax consistent and easier to understand for first-time programmers.


Authorship:

Ihsan Buker: original developer of package, current contributor, maintainer, and cleaner-upper of messes
Dr. Samantha Seals: current developer and maker of messes.

Links:

GitHub repo (with install instructions): http://github.com/sealslab/ssstats
Course implementations:

STA4173 - Biostatistics - Fall 2025
STA4231/STA6232 - Statistics for Data Science II

title: mean_median()
text: The mean_median() function provides the mean, standard deviation, median, and IQR of specified continuous variables.

title: mean_median()
text: The mean_median() function provides the mean, standard deviation, median, and IQR of specified continuous variables.

title: mean_median()
text:
Arguments



Argument
Type
Description




var_1
numeric
the first variable for description


var_2
numeric
the second variable for description


…
…
…


var_k
numeric
the kth variable for description

title: mean_median()
text:
Example 1

productivity %>% 
  mean_median(hours_sleep, cups_coffee)

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  variable    mean_sd   median_iqr
  <chr>       <chr>     <chr>     
1 cups_coffee 2.5 (1.8) 2.0 (2.0) 
2 hours_sleep 7.0 (1.5) 7.0 (2.0)

title: mean_median()
text:
Example 2

productivity %>% 
  group_by(character) %>%
  mean_median(hours_sleep, cups_coffee)

# A tibble: 12 × 4
   character variable    mean_sd   median_iqr
   <fct>     <chr>       <chr>     <chr>     
 1 Daisy     cups_coffee 2.0 (1.5) 2.0 (2.0) 
 2 Donald    cups_coffee 3.9 (2.0) 4.0 (3.0) 
 3 Goofy     cups_coffee 3.1 (1.8) 3.0 (2.0) 
 4 Mickey    cups_coffee 2.1 (1.4) 2.0 (2.0) 
 5 Minnie    cups_coffee 2.0 (1.4) 2.0 (2.0) 
 6 Pluto     cups_coffee 2.0 (1.4) 2.0 (2.0) 
 7 Daisy     hours_sleep 7.0 (1.5) 7.0 (2.0) 
 8 Donald    hours_sleep 7.1 (1.6) 7.0 (2.1) 
 9 Goofy     hours_sleep 7.0 (1.6) 6.9 (2.2) 
10 Mickey    hours_sleep 7.0 (1.5) 7.1 (1.9) 
11 Minnie    hours_sleep 7.0 (1.5) 7.1 (2.1) 
12 Pluto     hours_sleep 6.9 (1.5) 6.9 (2.0)

title: Example Data
text:
Researchers are interested in understanding what factors influence daily productivity and well-being among members of the Mickey and Friends community. Each observation in this dataset represents a single day in the life of one character. The goal of this study is to explore how sleep, mood, environment, and daily experiences contribute to productivity and interpersonal outcomes.
The dataset contains the following variables:

character: Mickey, Minnie, Donald, Daisy, Goofy, Pluto
role_type: Leader, Creative, Chaotic, Support
day_of_week: Monday – Sunday
weather: Sunny, Cloudy, Rainy, Windy
hours_sleep: Number of hours of sleep the character got the previous night
cups_coffee: Number of cups of coffee consumed that day
mood_score: Self-reported mood on a scale from 1 (very poor) to 10 (excellent)
energy_level: Self-reported energy level on a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high)
tasks_completed: Number of productive tasks completed that day
mishaps: Number of mistakes, accidents, or unexpected problems encountered
had_argument: Whether the character had an argument with someone that day (0 = No, 1 = Yes)
productivity_score: A continuous measure of overall productivity (higher values indicate greater productivity)


load("/Users/sseals/Desktop/GitHub/sealslab/ssstats/docs-src/files/productivity.RData")
head(productivity)

  character role_type day_of_week weather hours_sleep cups_coffee energy_level
1     Daisy  Creative         Mon   Rainy    8.205495           3          9.8
2    Minnie  Creative         Tue   Sunny    8.212625           2         10.0
3    Minnie  Creative         Sat   Sunny    6.628013           1          8.1
4    Minnie  Creative         Mon   Sunny    9.678781           2          9.7
5     Goofy   Chaotic         Thu   Sunny    5.752713           4          8.6
6     Daisy  Creative         Mon   Sunny    7.122821           4         10.0
  mood_score mishaps tasks_completed had_argument productivity_score
1        7.6       0              71            0             202.12
2        9.3       0              75            0             218.36
3       10.0       0              69            0             193.03
4       10.0       0              80            0             228.35
5        9.9       0              66            0             195.07
6        7.0       1              49            1             163.35

title: Tutorials for ssstats
text:
Class-specific:

STA4173 (Biostatistics)
STA4231 and STA6262 (Statistics for Data Science II)

Alphabetical list:

title: n_pct()
text: The n_pct() function provides a frequency table for one variable or an R \(\times\) C contingency table for two variables.

title: n_pct()
text:
Arguments








Argument
Type
Description




var_1 (required)
numeric
the first variable for description


var_2 (optional)
numeric
the second variable for description


denom (optional)
–
“col” (default) provides column frequencies while “row” provides row frequencies in contingency tables.

title: n_pct()
text:
Example 1

productivity %>% 
  n_pct(role_type)

 role_type      n (pct)
   Chaotic 1687 (33.7%)
  Creative 1637 (32.7%)
    Leader  846 (16.9%)
   Support  830 (16.6%)

title: n_pct()
text:
Example 2

productivity %>% 
  n_pct(weather, role_type)

# A tibble: 4 × 5
  weather Chaotic     Creative    Leader      Support    
  <fct>   <chr>       <chr>       <chr>       <chr>      
1 Cloudy  501 (29.7%) 514 (31.4%) 258 (30.5%) 245 (29.5%)
2 Rainy   342 (20.3%) 322 (19.7%) 159 (18.8%) 157 (18.9%)
3 Sunny   680 (40.3%) 644 (39.3%) 348 (41.1%) 356 (42.9%)
4 Windy   164 (9.7%)  157 (9.6%)  81 (9.6%)   72 (8.7%)

title: n_pct()
text:
Example 3

productivity %>% 
  n_pct(weather, role_type, denom = "row")

# A tibble: 4 × 5
  weather Chaotic     Creative    Leader      Support    
  <fct>   <chr>       <chr>       <chr>       <chr>      
1 Cloudy  501 (33.0%) 514 (33.9%) 258 (17.0%) 245 (16.1%)
2 Rainy   342 (34.9%) 322 (32.9%) 159 (16.2%) 157 (16.0%)
3 Sunny   680 (33.5%) 644 (31.8%) 348 (17.2%) 356 (17.6%)
4 Windy   164 (34.6%) 157 (33.1%) 81 (17.1%)  72 (15.2%)

title: table_one()
text: The table_one() function provides a customizable table of descriptive statistics, similar to a traditional Table 1 in biostatistics and epidemiology.

title: table_one()
text:
Arguments








Argument
Type
Description




cont_vars
list
a list of continuous variables for description


cat_vars
list
a list of categorical variables for description


split_var
variable name
the name of the variable to “split” the table on


cont_summary
–
“mean” (default) or “median”


cat_pct
–
“col” (default) or “row”


show_miss_cont
logical
TRUE or FALSE (default); toggle for the count of missing observations in the continuous variables


show_miss_cat
logical
TRUE or FALSE (default); toggle for the count of missing observations in the categorical variables


digits_cont
numeric
the number of decimal places to display for descriptions of continuous variables (default = 2)


digits_cat
numeric
the number of decimal places to display for descriptions of categorical variables (default = 1)



If working in Quarto or R Markdown, kable can be used to format tables.

title: table_one()
text:
Example 1

productivity %>% 
  table_one(cont_vars = c(hours_sleep, cups_coffee, energy_level),
            cat_vars = c(role_type))

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  variable       level      `Overall (n=5000)`
  <chr>          <chr>      <chr>             
1 "hours_sleep"  ""         "7 (1.53)"        
2 "cups_coffee"  ""         "2.52 (1.75)"     
3 "energy_level" ""         "8.47 (1.3)"      
4 "role_type"    ""         ""                
5 ""             "Chaotic"  "1687 (33.7%)"    
6 ""             "Creative" "1637 (32.7%)"    
7 ""             "Leader"   "846 (16.9%)"     
8 ""             "Support"  "830 (16.6%)"

title: table_one()
text:
Example 2

productivity %>% 
  table_one(cont_vars = c(hours_sleep, cups_coffee, energy_level),
            cat_vars = c(role_type),
            cont_summary = "median")

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  variable       level      `Overall (n=5000)` 
  <chr>          <chr>      <chr>              
1 "hours_sleep"  ""         "7.01 (5.98, 8.01)"
2 "cups_coffee"  ""         "2 (1, 3)"         
3 "energy_level" ""         "8.6 (7.5, 9.7)"   
4 "role_type"    ""         ""                 
5 ""             "Chaotic"  "1687 (33.7%)"     
6 ""             "Creative" "1637 (32.7%)"     
7 ""             "Leader"   "846 (16.9%)"      
8 ""             "Support"  "830 (16.6%)"

title: table_one()
text:
Example 4

kable(productivity %>% 
  table_one(cont_vars = c(hours_sleep, cups_coffee, energy_level),
            cat_vars = c(role_type),
            split_var = "had_argument"))

title: one_mean()
text: The one_mean() function returns:

title: one_mean()
text:
Arguments








Argument
Type
Description




outcome
numeric and continuous
the variable to make inference on


mu
numeric
hypothesized value for hypotheses (default = 0)


alternative
–
the direction of the hypothesis test; “two.sided” (default), “two”, “less”, or “greater”


alpha
numeric
specified \(\alpha\) (default = 0.05)

title: one_mean()
text:
Example 1

productivity %>% 
  one_mean(cups_coffee,
           mu = 2)

One-sample mean

x̅ = 2.5152 (SD = 1.7512)

95% CI for μ: (2.4666, 2.5638)

Hypotheses:
H₀: μ = 2
H₁: μ ≠ 2
Test statistic: t(4999) = 20.803
p-value: < 0.001
Conclusion: Reject the null hypothesis (p = < 0.001 < α = 0.050)

title: one_mean()
text:
Example 2

productivity %>% 
  one_mean(cups_coffee,
           mu = 2,
           alternative = "greater")

One-sample mean

x̅ = 2.5152 (SD = 1.7512)

95% CI for μ: (2.4666, 2.5638)

Hypotheses:
H₀: μ ≤ 2
H₁: μ > 2
Test statistic: t(4999) = 20.803
p-value: < 0.001
Conclusion: Reject the null hypothesis (p = < 0.001 < α = 0.050)

title: one_mean()
text:
Example 3

productivity %>% 
  one_mean(cups_coffee,
           mu = 2,
           alpha = 0.1)

One-sample mean

x̅ = 2.5152 (SD = 1.7512)

90% CI for μ: (2.4745, 2.5559)

Hypotheses:
H₀: μ = 2
H₁: μ ≠ 2
Test statistic: t(4999) = 20.803
p-value: < 0.001
Conclusion: Reject the null hypothesis (p = < 0.001 < α = 0.100)

title: one_mean()
text:
Related Functions
Users may also be interested in the one_qq_plot() function to assess for normality.
